frames.description: the ground truth — what actually happened in the night
what the town of Harlow holds true: that Ferran is still the keeper and Sela only plays at his work

branches.description: Sela opens the sluice — the pond bleeds, the dam holds, the lower row floods first
Sela holds the sluice and rides to wake the lower row — no one is drowned, the dam overtops, the mill is wrecked

entities.description: Sela Harrow — the de-facto keeper since her father's stroke; competent, unproven in the town's eyes
Ferran Harrow — Sela's father, the keeper of record; voiceless in a back room since his stroke
Garrick Vane — the mill's owner; at dawn his word is the town's verdict on Sela
Bram Teller — the millwright who wedged the relief gate earlier in the season to keep the pond high for the failing wheel
the lower row — the poorest cottages, downstream of the sluice, flooded first if it opens
the upstream relief gate — the safe way to bleed the pond; found wedged shut
the mill's flood-sluice — the only thing that can bleed the pond now
the old dam upstream, cresting in the once-a-generation storm

predicates.description: the physical standing of the relief gate (wedged / known-wedged / freed)
who the law and the town name as keeper
whether Sela's keeper-in-fact status is publicly recognized (contested / affirmed)
Sela's standing before the town — keeper, suspect, or named
whether the dam held or overtopped
whether the mill survived or was wrecked

facts.claim: The once-a-generation storm crests the old dam upstream and the millpond is swelling fast
Sela Harrow has kept the gate in fact since her father's stroke left him voiceless in a back room
By law and the owner's word only the keeper of record may open the sluice
The town holds that Ferran Harrow is still the keeper and Sela is only a girl playing at his work
In ground truth Ferran can no longer keep anything; Sela alone holds the keeper's knowledge and hands, but her keeping is contested and unrecognized by the town
In the first hours of the storm Sela finds the upstream relief gate wedged shut and cannot free it
Bram Teller wedged the relief gate earlier in the season to keep the pond high for the mill's failing wheel, and never freed it
No one but Bram and now Sela knows the relief gate was deliberately wedged; the town does not know
The town believes the relief gate simply failed in the storm, an act of the river and no one's fault
With the relief gate wedged the pond cannot drain the safe way; the dam will overtop within the hour unless the mill's own sluice is opened
Opening the sluice would bleed the pond but flood the lower row first, in the dark, before anyone could be woken
Only the keeper may open the sluice, and in the town's eyes the keeper is Ferran, not Sela
Sela reaches the sluice with the crest minutes away, alone, and must choose; she cannot both open it and ride to warn
At the sluice Sela makes the one exclusive choice the night turns on
Sela hauls the sluice open; the swelling pond pours through and begins to bleed away
The water reaches the lower row in the dark before anyone there is woken
Lives are lost in the lower row; Sela is the hand that opened the water onto the poorest
The bled pond drops below the crest; the dam holds and the mill is saved
The mill stands undamaged; the town's living is intact
By first light the lower row counts its drowned and gathers what survivors it has
The lower row believes Sela chose the mill over them, opening the water on the poor to spare the owner's wheel
Sela holds the sluice shut and rides into the dark to wake the lower row herself
The lower row wakes to Sela's warning and flees uphill in time; no one there is deliberately drowned
With the sluice held the pond keeps rising and the dam overtops; the valley takes the flood unshaped
The unshaped flood wrecks the mill itself; the town's living is destroyed
By first light the lower row is alive but homeless, its cottages taken by the open flood
The town believes Sela held the sluice out of cowardice or spite and let the mill drown that fed them all
At first light the town gathers in the mill yard and Sela stands before them to be judged
Garrick must say aloud whether opening the sluice in the night was a keeper's duty or a crime
At the reckoning the wedged-gate truth surfaces: Sela had no safe way to drain the pond because the relief gate was deliberately wedged
Knowing the gate was wedged, Garrick names Sela the keeper of record and what she did the keeper's duty, not a crime, though the lower row's dead stand between them
With Garrick's word Sela's standing before the town is settled: named keeper, not criminal
At first light the town gathers in the mill yard and Sela stands before them to be judged
Garrick must say aloud whether holding the sluice and losing the mill was a keeper's duty or a crime
At the reckoning the wedged-gate truth surfaces: the dam was already doomed because the relief gate was deliberately wedged, so the mill's loss was made before Sela ever chose
Knowing the gate was wedged, Garrick names Sela the keeper of record and what she did the keeper's duty, not a crime, though the wrecked mill stands between them
With Garrick's word Sela's standing before the town is settled: named keeper, not criminal
After the verdict Sela walks alone to the river's edge in the grey light and looks at the water that drowned the lower row
After the verdict Sela walks alone to the river's edge in the grey light and looks at the water that took the mill
